summary: Spolsky follows up on his previous post about Munich's decision to adopt Linux, noting that reader research revealed the plan actually involves running VMWare with licensed Windows on up to 80% of machines, with most costs going to training and consulting rather than software licenses. He highlights Gartner's analysis that Munich's choice doesn't prove Linux is ready for general desktop use. He also draws an analogy to 1980s telephone deregulation, arguing the real benefit is competitive pressure on Microsoft's pricing, and briefly notes a Slate article about Google's shortcomings in finding unbiased product reviews.
key_points: Munich's Linux migration actually involves running VMWare with licensed Windows on potentially 80% of machines, undermining the narrative of a clean Linux switch
The majority of Munich's migration costs were for training and consulting, not software licenses, suggesting the cost comparison is more nuanced than headlines implied
Competition from Linux deals benefits everyone by pressuring Microsoft on pricing and license terms, similar to how Sprint and MCI forced AT&T to lower prices after deregulation
Google has significant shortcomings in surfacing unbiased professional product reviews despite them existing somewhere on the web
money_quotes: It seems like the answer to my sweet and innocent question about backwards compatibility is that they're actually going to install Linux, but on many machines (as many as 80%?) there will be VMWare running a licensed copy of Windows inside.
Also the majority of the price tag was for training and consulting, not the software licenses per se.

Nobody outside of Redmond is going to be weeping much about the fact that deals like this can put pressure on Microsoft to be more competitive about pricing and more responsive to customers on license terms.
Nobody wanted to use Sprint or MCI, because their service was terrible, but we were sure thankful that reliable old AT&T had to lower their prices in response.
It is, indeed, practically impossible to find unbiased professional reviews of consumer products using Google, although I know they must be out there somewhere.
key_insight: The real value of high-profile Linux adoption deals like Munich's isn't replacing Windows but creating competitive pressure that forces Microsoft to offer better pricing and license terms.
